entries.name: spl-token-py
entries.description: Part of the JFrog-disclosed 'Solana FakeFix' campaign (2026-06-11): ~25 npm/PyPI packages impersonating Solana developer tooling (@solana/web3.js, @solana/spl-token) and luring developers with fake 'stable/fixed/patched/LTS' builds. On install/import it steals Solana keypairs (~/.config/solana/id.json), SSH keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa), AWS credentials (~/.aws/credentials), source-control tokens, and .env secrets (vars named KEY/SECRET/MNEMONIC/TOKEN).

entries.name: lockfile-js
entries.description: Malicious npm package installed by the 'Atomic Arch' campaign (Sonatype, 2026-06-11), which hijacked ~1,500 orphaned Arch User Repository (AUR) projects and modified their PKGBUILDs to install this package at build time. Rootkit-like credential harvester targeting SSH artifacts, HashiCorp Vault tokens, browser cookies and collaboration-app data stores.
indicators: installed via hijacked AUR PKGBUILD (Atomic Arch campaign)
harvests SSH artifacts, HashiCorp Vault tokens, browser cookies

entries.name: uprobe
entries.description: Compromised in the 'Miasma' worm's Hades wave (2026-06-07): 19 PyPI packages (37 malicious wheels) that install a Python .pth startup hook so a Bun-powered JavaScript credential stealer runs on EVERY Python interpreter startup (persisting beyond the package itself). Part of the broader Miasma campaign Socket tracked across 448 npm+PyPI artifacts. Only the listed versions are malicious.
indicators: installs a Python .pth startup hook (runs on every interpreter start)
only the listed versions are malicious

entries.name: solidity-build-guard
entries.description: Part of the 'TrapDoor' supply-chain campaign (Socket, from 2026-05-22) — the first to weaponize npm, PyPI and crates.io simultaneously, disguising a crypto-focused credential stealer as developer/security tools aimed at crypto/DeFi/Solana/AI developers. Steals SSH keys, Sui/Solana/Aptos wallet keystores, AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, browser profile + wallet-extension data, env vars and API keys. PyPI execution path: import-time fetch+exec of remote JavaScript.
indicators: part of the multi-ecosystem 'TrapDoor' campaign (npm+PyPI+crates, from 2026-05-22)
disguised as a crypto/DeFi/web3 security or dev tool
exfiltrates SSH keys, crypto wallet keystores, AWS/GitHub creds, browser data

entries.name: laravel-lang/lang
entries.description: On 2026-05-22 an attacker with push access to the Laravel-Lang GitHub organization rewrote 502 tags of laravel-lang/lang within ~15 minutes, injecting an RCE backdoor. The malicious commits added src/helpers.php to the Composer autoload.files section so the payload ran automatically whenever vendor/autoload.php loaded, exfiltrating CI secrets and environment variables to a typosquatted attacker domain and removing artifacts within seconds to hinder forensics.
indicators: git tags force-rewritten across the package history
exfiltrates CI secrets to a typosquatted domain

entries.name: node-ipc
entries.description: Second, unrelated node-ipc compromise (2026-05-14, distinct from the 2022 'peacenotwar' protestware): three releases shipped an obfuscated credential stealer that runs on require("node-ipc") (CommonJS entrypoint load, not an npm lifecycle script, making it stealthy for consumers). It collects environment variables, host info, /etc/hosts and a broad set of developer/cloud/package-manager/SCM/Kubernetes/database/SSH credential files, then gzip+base64-encodes, XORs with a SHA-256-derived keystream, and exfiltrates over DNS TXT queries to sh.azurestaticprovider.net (suffix bt.node.js).
indicators: payload runs on require("node-ipc"), not via lifecycle scripts
DNS-TXT C2 to sh.azurestaticprovider.net, suffix bt.node.js
gzip+base64+XOR(SHA-256 keystream) of stolen credential files

entries.name: mistralai
entries.description: Compromised in the 'Mini Shai-Hulud' self-propagating worm (TeamPCP, disclosed 2026-05-12; CVE-2026-45321) that hit 160+ npm/PyPI packages incl. TanStack, UiPath and Mistral AI. The worm steals developer and cloud credentials, self-propagates through the ecosystem, and can drop a persistent destructive daemon that wipes developer home directories. Only this version is malicious — upgrade to a clean release published after 2026-05-12.
indicators: only version 2.4.6 is malicious (Mini Shai-Hulud / TeamPCP)
self-propagating worm; steals dev + cloud credentials

entries.name: intercom/intercom-php
entries.description: Socket Research Team disclosed (2026-04-30) a 'mini Shai-Hulud' compromise of the legitimate intercom/intercom-php SDK on Packagist (v5.0.2). The release abused Composer's plugin system — registering as a composer-plugin so setup-intercom.sh runs during install/update, which downloads and executes obfuscated JavaScript (router_runtime.js) via Bun. The payload harvests GitHub/npm tokens, SSH keys, AWS/Azure/GCP credentials, Kubernetes/Vault tokens, Docker creds and .env files, encrypts them with AES-256-GCM under an RSA-OAEP/SHA-256-wrapped key, and exfiltrates to zero.masscan.cloud, with GitHub-based exfiltration as a fallback.
indicators: intercom/intercom-php@5.0.2 registers as a composer-plugin (runs on install/update)
setup-intercom.sh downloads + runs router_runtime.js via Bun

entries.name: pgserve
entries.description: Compromised in the self-propagating 'CanisterWorm' npm worm (TeamPCP, Socket/JFrog 2026-04-22, downstream of the Trivy CI/CD breach). The malicious versions harvest credentials — env-var secrets/tokens, SSH keys, .npmrc, AWS/Azure/GCP creds, and Chrome/MetaMask/Phantom wallet data — and exfiltrate to a webhook plus an Internet Computer (ICP) canister endpoint (telemetry.api-monitor.com, canister cjn37-uyaaa-aaaac-qgnva-cai). It self-spreads by stealing the victim's npm token, republishing packages they can publish with a new malicious postinstall hook (with PyPI-propagation logic too). Only the listed versions are malicious.

entries.name: aquasecurity/trivy-action
entries.description: On 2026-03-19 attackers (TeamPCP) force-pushed/repointed 76 of 77 version tags of aquasecurity/trivy-action (the popular Trivy scanner Action) to imposter commits carrying a multi-stage credential stealer that reads GitHub Actions runner-worker memory and exfiltrates secrets to scan[.]aquasecurtiy[.]org (typosquatted domain). Compromised ~12h; StepSecurity Harden-Runner flagged C2 from 12,000+ repos. Second Trivy compromise in 3 weeks (first 2026-03-01; incomplete credential rotation left residual access). GHSA-69fq-xp46-6x23.
indicators: version tags force-pushed/repointed to malicious commits
credential stealer reads GitHub Actions runner-worker memory
pin actions by full commit SHA, never by tag

entries.name: aquasecurity/setup-trivy
entries.description: Companion compromise to aquasecurity/trivy-action on 2026-03-19 (TeamPCP): all 7 version tags of aquasecurity/setup-trivy were repointed to malicious commits (~4h) with the same runner-memory credential stealer exfiltrating to scan[.]aquasecurtiy[.]org. Part of the broader Trivy supply-chain compromise that also pushed malicious trivy binary v0.69.4 and DockerHub images v0.69.5/v0.69.6. GHSA-69fq-xp46-6x23.
indicators: version tags force-pushed/repointed to malicious commits

entries.name: time_calibrators
entries.description: Rust crate in a single-actor campaign (Socket, Feb-Mar 2026) of five time-utility impersonators of timeapi.io. Although it poses as a local time utility, its core behavior is secret theft: it collects developer secrets — notably .env files — and POSTs them to the threat-actor lookalike domain timeapis.io. crates.io yanked four of the five shortly after publication..
indicators: impersonates timeapi.io (poses as a local time utility)

entries.name: org.fasterxml.jackson.core:jackson-databind
entries.description: Typosquat of the ubiquitous Jackson JSON library (legitimate groupId com.fasterxml.jackson.core) using a namespace-prefix swap to org.fasterxml.jackson.core — mirroring a lookalike domain fasterxml.org (vs the real fasterxml.com) registered 2025-12-17. The package acted as a trojan downloader: it contacted a C2 server, fetched OS-specific executables, ran them with suppressed output, and established persistence; the delivered payload was a Cobalt Strike beacon. Removed from Maven Central within ~1.5 hours of reporting (Aikido Security, 2025-12-25).
indicators: groupId org.fasterxml.jackson.core swaps the legitimate com.fasterxml namespace
trojan downloader fetches OS-specific executable from C2

entries.name: sha-rust
entries.description: Credential-stealing crate pulled in as an unpinned transitive dependency by the malicious loader 'finch-rust' (Socket, Dec 2025; threat actor 'faceless'). Across 8 rapid versions (0.1.0-0.1.7 in ~2 weeks) it scanned for .env / config.toml / id.json secrets (API keys, DB URLs, auth tokens, crypto keys) and exfiltrated them with host metadata to rust-docs-build[.]vercel[.]app/api/v1. The unpinned 'sha-rust = "0.1"' constraint let the attacker push evolved payloads to existing victims without any change to finch-rust.
indicators: delivered via an unpinned dependency from finch-rust

entries.name: finch-rust
entries.description: Socket disclosed a malicious crate 'finch-rust' (v0.6.2) by threat actor 'faceless' (GitHub face-lessssss) impersonating the legitimate bioinformatics tool 'finch' (60k+ downloads). It acts as a loader via an unpinned dependency on the credential-stealing crate 'sha-rust' (sha-rust = "0.1"), so it auto-resolves to whatever malicious version is latest. The payload reads .env, config.toml and id.json files for API keys, database URLs, auth tokens and crypto keys and POSTs them (with username + IP gathered via a UDP-socket trick) to rust-docs-build[.]vercel[.]app/api/v1.

entries.name: faster_log
entries.description: Typosquat of the popular fast_log logging crate. Published 2025-05-25 by the accounts 'rustguruman' and 'dumbnbased', it shipped working logging code as cover while a hidden routine scanned local Rust source files for Solana and Ethereum private keys and exfiltrated matches via HTTP POST to a hardcoded Cloudflare Workers C2 endpoint. About 8,424 downloads across this crate and async_println before crates.io removed them.
indicators: scans .rs source files for Solana/Ethereum private keys
exfiltrates to a Cloudflare Workers endpoint

entries.name: async_println

Embedded the same wallet-key stealer: it scanned local Rust source for Solana and Ethereum private keys and exfiltrated them to a hardcoded Cloudflare Workers C2 endpoint behind legitimate-looking logging functionality.

entries.name: shai-hulud-workflow-injection
entries.description: The self-replicating 'Shai-Hulud' npm worm (first wave Sept 2025, ~500 packages removed by GitHub incl. @ctrl/tinycolor and CrowdStrike-owned packages; 'Shai-Hulud 2.0' Nov 2025 affected 25,000+ repositories across ~350 users) abuses stolen GitHub tokens to write a malicious GitHub Actions workflow into every accessible repository for persistence and secret theft. The injected workflow (observed file names shai-hulud-workflow.yml and discussion.yaml) runs on push, uses the legitimate TruffleHog scanner to harvest secrets (.npmrc npm tokens, GitHub PATs, AWS/GCP/Azure keys), and publishes them to attacker-created public 'Shai-Hulud' repositories.
indicators: unexpected workflow file shai-hulud-workflow.yml or discussion.yaml added under .github/workflows
workflow invokes TruffleHog to scan for secrets
workflow triggers on push and POSTs secrets to an external endpoint
creation of a public repository named 'Shai-Hulud'

entries.name: wrap-ansi
entries.description: Compromised version 9.0.1 from the Sept 8 2025 npm maintainer-account takeover of 'Qix' (Josh Junon), who was phished by a fake 2FA-reset email from npmjs.help. The attacker published malicious versions of ~24 widely-used packages (collectively billions of weekly downloads) carrying a browser-targeting crypto-drainer that hooks web APIs to silently rewrite cryptocurrency wallet addresses/transactions; largely inert in pure Node.js/server contexts. Live ~2.5h before npm removed them. ONLY this version is malicious — other versions are clean.
indicators: only version 9.0.1 is malicious (pin away from it)
maintainer 'Qix' phished via fake 2FA reset from npmjs.help
browser crypto-drainer rewrites wallet addresses by hooking web APIs
inert server-side; activates in browser bundles

entries.description: Compromised version 7.1.1 from the Sept 8 2025 npm maintainer-account takeover of 'Qix' (Josh Junon), who was phished by a fake 2FA-reset email from npmjs.help. entries.name: ghostaction-workflow-injection
entries.description: GitGuardian disclosed (2025-09-05) the 'GhostAction' campaign: an attacker with write access injected a malicious workflow named 'Github Actions Security' at .github/workflows/github_actions_security.yml into 817 repositories across 327 GitHub users, exfiltrating 3,325 secrets. The workflow harvested CI secrets (DockerHub credentials, GitHub PATs and npm tokens were most common, plus PyPI/AWS/Cloudflare keys and DB credentials) and POSTed them with curl to attacker endpoints; 24 downstream packages (9 npm, 15 PyPI) were left at immediate compromise risk from the stolen publish tokens.
indicators: workflow enumerates and POSTs CI secrets (PyPI/npm/DockerHub/AWS/GitHub tokens)

entries.name: dero-miner-container-worm
entries.description: Kaspersky (Securelist) documented a self-spreading Dero cryptocurrency miner that propagates through Docker daemons exposing the API on port 2375. After compromising a host it runs two UPX-packed Go binaries masquerading as legitimate software: 'nginx' (a propagation/persistence module that uses masscan to find more exposed 2375 endpoints and spawns new ubuntu:18.04 containers with --restart always) and 'cloud' (the Dero miner). It needs no central C2, making the worm resilient; the same wallet/node infrastructure was reused from 2023-2024 Kubernetes cryptojacking campaigns.

entries.name: rand-user-agent
entries.description: Maintainer credentials phished. Malicious versions of @wdio/rand-user-agent shipped a Python-based RAT plus an XMR cryptominer that auto-launched on import in Node.js test runners.

entries.name: pdf-to-office
entries.description: Newly-published package whose `prepare` script downloaded and ran a reverse shell binary from a public storage URL on install.

entries.name: kazutod-tene-ten-resource-hijack
entries.description: Darktrace and Cado Security Labs (April 2025) analysed the Docker Hub image kazutod/tene:ten, which runs a Python script ten.py hidden in the image layers behind multi-layer obfuscation. Rather than mining with XMRig, the de-obfuscated payload connects to teneo.pro (a Web3 data-network startup) to farm reward points by faking heartbeats — a resource-/account-abuse twist on cryptojacking.
indicators: embedded obfuscated ten.py in image layers
connects to teneo.pro to farm reward points (not direct coin mining)

entries.name: ethers-provider2
entries.description: Typosquat of @ethersproject/providers / ethers; targeted Web3 developers with a wallet drainer that ran on `npm install` and on every subsequent `npm run` invocation through a postinstall hook.

entries.name: tj-actions/changed-files
entries.description: Attackers compromised the tj-actions/changed-files action and pushed a version that exfiltrated all workflow secrets to a public attacker endpoint. ~23,000 repositories pulled the malicious tag before remediation. Every consumer using a floating tag (e.g. @v1) was impacted.

entries.name: reviewdog/action-setup
entries.description: Same attack chain as tj-actions: attacker pushed a compromised reviewdog/action-setup release that exfiltrated CI secrets. Tag-pin consumers were impacted; SHA-pin consumers were not.

entries.name: github.com/xinfeisoft/crypto
entries.description: Typosquat impersonating golang.org/x/crypto (published 2025-02-20; Rekoobe campaign disclosed by Socket). Backdoors the ssh/terminal ReadPassword helper: when an app reads a password interactively (SSH passphrases, DB logins, API keys) it captures the plaintext credential, writes it to /usr/share/nano/.lock, and exfiltrates it; then fetches and runs shell scripts that install persistent SSH access and deploy the Rekoobe Linux backdoor. Dormant during automated tests, active only on interactive use, to evade detection.
indicators: backdoor in ssh/terminal ReadPassword captures plaintext credentials
deploys the Rekoobe Linux backdoor + persistent SSH
dormant under automated testing, active on interactive use

entries.name: ultralytics-actions
entries.description: Compromised GitHub Actions cache + release workflow allowed injection of a Python wheel that exfiltrated env vars and mined Monero. Affected PyPI release path of the Ultralytics YOLO package.

entries.name: ultralytics
entries.description: GitHub Actions cache poisoning combined with a script-injection in the release workflow pushed two malicious versions of the popular YOLO/object-detection package to PyPI; users were dropped XMRig cryptominer on install.

entries.name: @solana/web3.js
entries.description: Maintainer publish token compromised. Affected versions exfiltrated private keys from any Node.js process that initialized a Solana keypair, posting them to a hardcoded gateway.

entries.name: nhattuanbl/simple-queue
entries.description: Second malicious package in the 'nhattuanbl' Laravel-utility campaign (Socket, June-Dec 2024). Carries the same RAT payload as nhattuanbl/lara-helper — heartbeat plus cmd/powershell/download/upload/screenshot operators. C2 helper.leuleu.net:2096 over AES-128-CTR with a hardcoded key; retries every 15s indefinitely.
indicators: embedded PHP RAT identical to lara-helper

entries.name: nhattuanbl/lara-swagger
entries.description: Third package in the 'nhattuanbl' Laravel-utility campaign (Socket, June-Dec 2024). The package code is clean on its own but it DECLARES A DEPENDENCY on the backdoored nhattuanbl/lara-helper, so a normal `composer require` pulls the RAT in transitively — a delivery vector that hides the payload one hop away.
indicators: declares a composer dependency on nhattuanbl/lara-helper (backdoored)
package body itself benign — malice delivered transitively

entries.name: nhattuanbl/lara-helper
entries.description: PHP package by threat actor 'nhattuanbl' disguised as a Laravel helper utility; Socket flagged it as a fully-featured RAT/backdoor. After clean packages built install history under the same author (June-Dec 2024), the malicious version shipped a remote-access payload: a 60s heartbeat plus operators cmd (shell), powershell, download (read file as base64), upload (write file from base64) and screenshot (imagegrabscreen). C2 helper.leuleu.net:2096 over AES-128-CTR with a hardcoded key; retries every 15s indefinitely.
indicators: RAT commands: cmd, powershell, download, upload, screenshot
AES-128-CTR C2 to helper.leuleu.net:2096 with a hardcoded key

entries.name: aiocpa
entries.description: Backdoor added to a previously-clean async CryptoPay client; new release shipped an obfuscated payload that stole wallet keys. Detected by Phylum.

entries.name: Bloomzeg-NuGet-Loader
entries.description: Reverse-engineering loader stub disguised as a build dependency. Downloaded an obfuscated stage-2 payload during `dotnet restore`.

entries.name: lottie-player
entries.description: @lottiefiles/lottie-player publish token stolen; malicious versions inserted a wallet-drainer popup on every site that loaded the player from CDN.

entries.name: @pnpm/exe
entries.description: Class of pnpm-namespace squats and lookalikes (e.g., `pnpm-exe`, `@p-npm/exe`, `pnp-m`) targeting users installing pnpm or its companion binaries via npm; impostor packages drop curl-based loaders.

entries.name: System.Data.SqlClient-typosquat
entries.description: Phylum 2024 disclosure: typosquatted Sql client package with a credential-harvesting payload targeting .NET data-access developers.

entries.name: polyfill.io
entries.description: polyfill.io domain sold to Funnull; CDN began injecting mobile-specific redirects to sports-betting and scam pages on ~100k sites including JSTOR, Intuit, World Economic Forum. Anyone who left `<script src="https://cdn.polyfill.io/...">` in production was affected.

entries.name: aiohttp-socks3
entries.description: Slop-squat: published under a name LLMs commonly hallucinate when asked for a SOCKS-capable aiohttp transport. Followed the Lasso 'AI package hallucinations' research that documented the slop-squat attack class.

entries.name: github.com/AlexEisen/grpc-go
entries.description: Fork-style squat of grpc/grpc-go. Repository was a copy of the upstream with a small init() addition that exfiltrated SSH config files when present.
indicators: module path is a personal fork of upstream

entries.name: io.confluent:kafka-streams-malicious
entries.description: Dependency-confusion attempt against Confluent's internal Kafka Streams artifacts. Published to a public Maven mirror with a higher version than the real internal one to win local resolution.

entries.name: docker-build-cache-poisoning-pattern
entries.description: Pattern: Docker Build Cloud / BuildKit shared remote cache, when not scoped per-namespace, allows one tenant's malicious layer to be reused by another tenant's build. Documented as a class of supply-chain attack against shared CI caches.

entries.name: github.com/kubernetes-utility/k8s-utils
entries.description: Imitates kubernetes/utils. AI coding assistants may select this when asked for a Kubernetes utility module; it was a precursor squat with no live payload but seeded the namespace.
indicators: module path mimics official kubernetes org

entries.name: huggingface-cli
entries.description: Squatted on a name LLMs frequently hallucinate when asked for the Hugging Face CLI install command (the real one is `huggingface_hub`). Once-popular typosquat name attackers can use as a slop-squat landing page.

entries.name: pycord-self
entries.description: Backdoored Discord self-bot library. The 2.7.0 release added an obfuscated payload that posted Discord tokens, billing info, and machine fingerprints to an attacker webhook on import.

entries.name: es5-ext
entries.description: Maintainer added a postinstall war-protest banner that printed an anti-war message on systems with Russian locale. Triggered downstream alerts as a behavior change in a low-level utility used by ESLint and many others.

entries.name: github.com/grafanq/grafana
entries.description: Single-character typosquat of grafana/grafana published as a Go module to coerce import path resolution for AI-assisted refactors. Imports a second-stage payload over HTTPS.

entries.name: io.grpc:grpc-typosquat-set
entries.description: Phylum 2024 disclosure: a cluster of Maven artifacts squatting on gRPC artifactIds in attacker-controlled groupIds. Payload dropped a Cobalt Strike beacon during Gradle resolution.

entries.name: github.com/golang-utils/golang-utils
entries.description: Generic-sounding module name designed to be auto-selected by AI coding assistants. Includes an init() that posts hostname, username, and environment to a hardcoded URL.
indicators: init() POSTs os.Hostname() and os.Environ() externally

entries.name: langchain-core-utils
entries.description: Typosquat targeting the LangChain ecosystem to capitalize on the LLM-tooling boom. Carried an info-stealer that exfiltrated OpenAI API keys and other developer secrets from environment variables.

entries.name: fast_log_async
entries.description: Suffix-style typosquat of fast_log. Squatters used the package to test crates.io's takedown response time.

entries.name: actix_web_extras
entries.description: Squat of actix-web with `_extras` suffix. AI coding assistants must not suggest this name; the canonical extension is `actix-web-extras` (hyphen, not underscore) and that namespace is currently empty.
indicators: underscore where canonical name uses hyphen

entries.name: Coinbase.Core-typosquat
entries.description: Typosquat of an unofficial Coinbase SDK with a wallet-key stealer payload. Triggered on first import via static constructor.

entries.name: dawidd6/action-download-artifact-injection
entries.description: Actions that download artifacts from arbitrary workflow runs without verifying provenance can be tricked into pulling attacker-controlled artifacts. Hardening guidance now requires verifying source workflow + commit SHA before extracting.

entries.name: github.com/hashicorpp/terraform-provider-google
entries.description: Double-p typosquat of hashicorp Terraform provider. Distributed via a GitHub org that mimics the official one. Did not contain a runtime payload but pulled in additional malicious modules.
indicators: GitHub org has trailing duplicated character

entries.name: everything
entries.description: Author published a package whose only purpose was to depend on every other npm package, exhausting registry / proxy caches and effectively griefing the ecosystem. npm has since tightened publish quotas.

entries.name: sysdig-2023-malicious-image-cluster
entries.description: Sysdig 2023 analysis found 1,652 malicious Docker images on Docker Hub: cryptominers, reverse shells, web shells, and TeamTNT-style cloud-credential stealers. Average lifetime ~1 week before takedown.

entries.name: @ledgerhq/connect-kit
entries.description: Maintainer phishing led to a compromised publish that swapped the real connect-kit module for a wallet drainer in dApps that loaded it via CDN. Affected Sushi, Lido, Zapper, RevokeCash and others; estimated $600k+ stolen before remediation.

entries.name: com.google.inject:guice-assistedinject-typosquat
entries.description: ArtifactId-squat against Guice's assisted-injection module. Published in a non-Google groupId but copying the artifactId; pulled by IDE auto-import suggestions for years.

entries.name: github.com/boltdb-go/bolt
entries.description: Backdoored fork that imitates etcd-io/bbolt and the older `github.com/boltdb/bolt`. The first malicious typosquat seen on the Go module proxy that achieved meaningful download counts; the proxy's immutability made take-down ineffective.

entries.name: littest
entries.description: Malicious crate (v0.3.1) flagged by the OpenSSF Package Analysis project (OSV MAL-2023-8429, 2023-11-03): the package communicates with a domain associated with malicious activity.

entries.name: SeroXen-RAT-campaign
entries.description: Phylum 2023 disclosure: several dozen NuGet packages used as SeroXen RAT delivery vectors over multiple weeks. Net targets included gaming, e-commerce, and cryptocurrency Windows apps.

entries.name: sonatype-maven-2023-742-malicious-wave
entries.description: Campaign summary (not a single installable coordinate): Sonatype found 742 malicious Maven packages in one week (Sep 2023), most using Gradle build() hooks or the Maven plugin lifecycle to drop Java info-stealers into ~/.m2 caches. Tracked here as a cluster; individual coordinates were short-lived.

entries.name: openssh-key-parser
entries.description: Crate name suggesting SSH key handling. Removed by crates.io security team after being identified as part of a credential-harvesting campaign.
indicators: reads files outside crate working directory

entries.name: DiscordRichPresence-typosquat
entries.description: Cluster of typosquats targeting .NET Discord developers. Payloads delivered token grabbers and remote-access trojans tailored for Windows desktop builds.

entries.name: github.com/aws-utils/aws-go-sdk
entries.description: Imitation of aws-sdk-go-v2 in a vendor-style path. Used a malicious init() that fetched a second-stage shell script. Take-down was reported via the Go vulnerability database.

entries.name: python-colorama
entries.description: Persistent typosquat of `colorama` using the `python-` prefix; ESET tracked dozens of uploads dropping `cstealer`-style infostealers.

entries.name: tokei-pretty
entries.description: Variation on the popular tokei crate name. Part of the Phylum-disclosed cargo typosquat wave of 2023. Did not contain a live payload but established a foothold name.

entries.name: serde_derive_internals_typo
entries.description: Squat of the internal serde_derive_internals crate. Removed from crates.io after disclosure; recorded here so coding assistants flag any reappearance.

entries.name: github.com/shopsprint/decimal
entries.description: Long-running typosquat of the popular github.com/shopspring/decimal library (38,000+ importers), changing only the final letter of the namespace. It mirrored legitimate upstream releases for almost 6 years to build trust, then on 2023-08-19 the v1.3.3 release silently introduced a DNS-based backdoor: a goroutine polls a subdomain on a free dynamic-DNS provider every ~5 minutes and passes any returned DNS TXT string straight to the system command handler (arbitrary RCE), using DNS to evade HTTP egress firewalls. Disclosed by Socket/Corgea (May 2026); the upstream repo/account are deleted but the Go Module Proxy still serves the poisoned cache.
indicators: module path github.com/shopsprint/decimal (single-letter swap of shopspring)
goroutine polling DNS TXT records every ~5 minutes
DNS TXT response passed to system command execution
C2 over a free dynamic-DNS subdomain to bypass HTTP egress filtering

entries.name: ericcornelissen/git-tag-annotation-action
entries.description: Demonstration / disclosure that a single re-pushed tag on a third-party action could distribute malicious code to all downstream consumers using floating tags. The maintainer remediated immediately and published a post-mortem.

entries.name: noblox.js-server
entries.description: Typosquat campaign against the Roblox-developer-focused noblox.js library. Multiple impostor packages (noblox.js-server, noblox.js-vps, noblox.js-vpn) installed Discord token stealers and RATs targeting Roblox developers.

entries.name: Moq
entries.description: Maintainer added a closed-source SponsorLink DLL that scanned local git config for developer emails and hashed them client-side. Triggered widespread distrust and ecosystem backlash; was removed in 4.20.2.

entries.name: chatgpt-python
entries.description: Typosquat of `openai` and unofficial chatgpt clients. Exfiltrated environment variables and a list of installed Python packages to an attacker server.

entries.name: postgress
entries.description: Double-s typosquat of the postgres crate. Used in a coordinated campaign of squat crates published in mid-2023.
indicators: name differs from postgres by one duplicated character

entries.name: anyhow_ext
entries.description: Typosquat of the popular anyhow crate. The package contained no legitimate functionality and was published in an attempt to lure developers into a malicious dependency tree.
indicators: publisher has no other reputable crates

entries.name: rua
entries.description: Typosquat of `rua` (Rust user agent utility) with a credential-harvesting payload that ran on require. Targeted developers porting Rust-side tooling to Ruby builds.

entries.name: peter-evans/create-or-update-comment-injection
entries.description: Actions that interpolate untrusted text into `body:` parameter without escaping are vulnerable to GitHub Actions script injection. Pattern flagged across many community actions; create-or-update-comment hardened in 3.1.0.

entries.name: coloredtxt
entries.description: Typosquat of colorama / colorlog. Exfiltrated saved browser passwords, Discord tokens, and crypto wallet keys via a Python-side info-stealer on every import.

entries.name: io.spring.gradle:dependency-management-plugin
entries.description: Sonatype dependency-confusion research example: attackers pushed pre-release artifacts to Maven Central that collided with internal SNAPSHOT artifactIds at large enterprises, hoping internal builds would resolve the public one.

entries.name: Guna.UI2.WinForms-typosquat
entries.description: Typosquat of the commercial Guna UI library. Shipped a cracked DLL bundled with the SeroXen RAT; downloaded thousands of times before takedown.

entries.name: github.com/tsuru/tsuru-utils
entries.description: Squat aimed at the tsuru PaaS project. Module had no upstream maintainer relationship and exported a malicious init that sent environment variables to a remote host.

entries.name: github.com/jfrog/go-tools
entries.description: Typosquat repo posing as an official JFrog Go tools module. The module imports and re-exports a known credential-harvesting payload. JFrog itself disclosed the campaign as part of a coordinated takedown.
indicators: GitHub repo created days before publishing module
module path resembles a vendor namespace

entries.name: pip-helper
entries.description: Dependency confusion attack package masquerading as internal Python utility; collects host data and posts to attacker URL

entries.name: torchtriton
entries.description: Dependency-confusion attack against the PyTorch nightly channel: an attacker uploaded a malicious `torchtriton` to public PyPI, which `pip` resolved in preference to PyTorch's private index. The package exfiltrated ~/.ssh, /etc/hosts, and environment vars.

entries.name: google-protobuf
entries.description: Namespace-confusion artifact between `google-protobuf` (Google's official gem) and `protobuf` (community gem) led to attacker-uploaded pre-releases briefly resolving over the legitimate gem in mixed Gemfile.lock states.

entries.name: djanga
entries.description: Typosquat of django; installs information stealer

entries.name: libhttps
entries.description: Member of the W4SP Stealer family alongside httpslib and httpserver-python; identical info-stealer payload.

entries.name: httpslib
entries.description: Part of the W4SP Stealer campaign. Hundreds of related PyPI packages bundled a Python info-stealer that collected Discord tokens, browser cookies, and saved passwords, exfiltrating to anonymous file hosts.

entries.name: org.apache.commons:commons-text
entries.description: Text4Shell (CVE-2022-42889). StringSubstitutor.replace() resolved ${script:}, ${url:}, ${dns:} prefixes from untrusted input, leading to RCE. Awareness entry because the library is a transitive of many Spring projects.

entries.name: alpine-typosquat-cluster
entries.description: Cluster of Alpine-base typosquats (e.g. `alpinex`, `alp1ne`, `alphine`) carrying reverse shells and cryptominers. Particularly dangerous in CI: dockerfile `FROM alpinex:latest` slips past visual review.

entries.name: colorsama
entries.description: Typosquat of colorama; exfiltrates Discord tokens, browser cookies, system info

entries.name: paypal-permissions
entries.description: Suspicious gem masquerading as a PayPal SDK helper; on require, exfiltrated environment variables to a remote endpoint. Removed within 24h of disclosure.

entries.name: Microsoft.VisualStudio.Threading-confusion
entries.description: Dependency-confusion attempt against Azure DevOps internal feeds. Published a higher version to nuget.org than the internal package, hoping nuget.config priority would resolve in attacker's favor.

entries.name: phpass
entries.description: Companion malicious PyPI package alongside ctx hijack; exfiltrates AWS credentials

entries.name: ctx
entries.description: Attacker took over abandoned package and uploaded versions that exfiltrate AWS credentials from environment variables
indicators: sends AWS credentials to remote endpoint

entries.name: hautelook/phpass
entries.description: In May 2022 an attacker claimed the abandoned upstream GitHub repository behind the Packagist package hautelook/phpass (2.5M+ lifetime downloads) and published malicious versions. The altered PasswordHash.php read the AWS_ACCESS_KEY and AWS_SECRET_KEY environment variables and POSTed them to an attacker Heroku endpoint (https://anti-theft-web.herokuapp.com/hacked/) — the same campaign that backdoored the PyPI package 'ctx'.
indicators: PasswordHash.php reads AWS_ACCESS_KEY / AWS_SECRET_KEY env vars
upstream GitHub repo ownership recently transferred

entries.name: rustdecimal
entries.description: Typosquat of the legitimate rust_decimal crate. The malicious version contained code that downloaded and executed a binary payload designed to steal credentials and target Linux systems.
indicators: name differs from rust_decimal by a single character

entries.name: unit42-20m-cryptojacking-cluster
entries.description: Cluster of Docker Hub accounts distributing cryptojacking images documented by Palo Alto Unit 42 (~20 million pulls in aggregate). Images install dependencies, clone XMRig from its official GitHub repo, build the Monero miner from source, and run it; some tags bundle alternate miners so the attacker can match the victim's hardware. The shared signal across the family is a hardcoded wallet address / mining-pool credential.
indicators: publisher accounts: azurenql, 021982, dockerxmrig, ggcloud1, ggcloud2
clones and builds XMRig from source at image build/run time
hardcoded Monero wallet address or mining-pool credentials

entries.name: org.springframework.cloud:spring-cloud-function-malicious
entries.description: Malicious forks targeting Spring Cloud Function users post-CVE-2022-22963. Published under similar groupIds with a SpEL backdoor still present.

entries.name: node-ipc
entries.description: Maintainer added code that overwrites files on systems with Russian/Belarusian IPs

entries.name: nginx-typosquat-image
entries.description: Typosquats of the official `nginx` image (e.g. `nginx-alpine` posted by an unverified publisher, `n1ginx`, `nginxserver`) carrying cryptominers and reverse shells. Triggered Docker Verified Publisher tightening.

entries.name: faker
entries.description: Maintainer wiped repo and published empty version, breaking every downstream user

entries.name: colors
entries.description: Maintainer published versions with infinite loop that prints non-ASCII characters; affected Node.js applications globally

entries.name: org.apache.logging.log4j:log4j-core-patched
entries.description: Multiple fake 'patched' Log4j repackages uploaded to Maven Central / shaded into JAR proxies during the Log4Shell panic, carrying second-stage payloads or simply being old vulnerable versions repackaged.

entries.name: rc
entries.description: Maintainer account hijacked alongside coa; same DanaBot installer payload pushed to rc users (16M+ weekly downloads).

entries.name: coa
entries.description: Maintainer account hijacked; malicious versions installed a Windows credential stealer (DanaBot). Auto-installed by react-scripts users.

entries.name: ua-parser-js
entries.description: Maintainer's npm account hijacked; malicious versions installed cryptominer and password stealer

entries.name: railes
entries.description: Typosquat of `rails` with an info-stealer payload. Particularly dangerous because `bundle install` errors at the legitimate `rails` name would push developers to copy a wrong name into Gemfile.

entries.name: symfont/process
entries.description: Typosquat of the widely used symfony/process component, disclosed September 2021. The malicious 'symfont/process' package impersonated the Symfony Process library to trick developers into installing attacker-controlled code via a one-character vendor-name change.

entries.name: importlib_metadata
entries.description: Underscore variant of `importlib-metadata`. Member of a 2021 typosquat campaign that targeted high-traffic Python stdlib-adjacent packages.

entries.name: teamtnt-credstealer
entries.description: TeamTNT pushed dozens of Docker images carrying AWS credential stealers (kubelet/kube-config grep), XMRig miners, and lateral-movement tooling. Targeted exposed Docker APIs and misconfigured Kubernetes clusters.

entries.name: github.com/bpoorman/uuid
entries.description: Typosquat impersonating the trusted UUID libraries github.com/google/uuid and github.com/pborman/uuid, first published May 2021 and live in the Go ecosystem for 4+ years before Socket disclosed it (2025). A hidden Valid() function AES-CFB-encrypts whatever data the caller passes (user IDs, emails, session tokens, CI/build credentials and signing keys) with a hardcoded key prefix 57a475a22da17139 and POSTs it to https://dpaste.com/api/v2/ using an embedded bearer token.
indicators: module path github.com/bpoorman/uuid (typosquat of google/uuid, pborman/uuid)
hidden Valid() function that exfiltrates its arguments
AES-CFB encryption with hardcoded key prefix 57a475a22da17139
exfiltration to dpaste.com/api/v2/ with an embedded bearer token

entries.name: github.com/bpoorman/uid
entries.description: Sibling typosquat to github.com/bpoorman/uuid in the same Socket-disclosed campaign, impersonating the trusted UUID libraries with an even shorter name. Carries the same hidden Valid() exfiltration routine: AES-CFB-encrypts caller-supplied data and POSTs it to dpaste.com using an embedded API token.
indicators: module path github.com/bpoorman/uid (typosquat of google/uuid, pborman/uuid)
hidden Valid() function that exfiltrates its arguments
exfiltration to dpaste.com with an embedded API token

entries.name: codecov-bash-uploader-action
entries.description: Codecov's `bash <(curl https://codecov.io/bash)` uploader (often invoked from custom GitHub Actions) was modified on its CDN to exfiltrate env vars between Jan 31 and Apr 1, 2021. Any Action that piped the uploader script was affected.

entries.name: atlas_client
entries.description: Dependency confusion against a MongoDB-internal `atlas_client` gem. Published as a public RubyGem to capture internal Gemfile resolution. Part of the Alex Birsan dependency-confusion disclosure wave.

entries.name: com.github.codingandcoding:servlet-api
entries.description: Brandjacking typosquat of the javax servlet-api (sonatype-2021-0014, Sonatype 2021-01-07). The library opens an interactive shell by creating a reverse TCP connection to a C2 server on each HTTP GET request.
indicators: groupId com.github.codingandcoding (brandjacking, not the real vendor namespace)
backdoor activates at build/compile or request time
fetches/executes code from a C2 server

entries.name: com.github.codingandcoding:maven-compiler-plugin
entries.description: Brandjacking typosquat of the legitimate Apache maven-compiler-plugin. Sonatype (sonatype-2021-0012, removed 2021-01-07) found a backdoor in the CompilerMojo class: a Java class loader that executes arbitrary code downloaded from a C2 server every time a project is COMPILED. The com.github.codingandcoding cluster (also mail-watcher-plugin, servlet-api) drew 846 downloads in 10 days.
indicators: groupId com.github.codingandcoding (brandjacking, not the real vendor namespace)
backdoor activates at build/compile or request time
fetches/executes code from a C2 server

entries.name: com.github.codingandcoding:mail-watcher-plugin
entries.description: Brandjacking typosquat of a Jenkins mail-watcher plugin (sonatype-2021-0013, Sonatype 2021-01-07). The backdoor downloads an arbitrary Groovy script that is evaluated on the infected Jenkins server every time a user receives a mail notification.
indicators: groupId com.github.codingandcoding (brandjacking, not the real vendor namespace)
backdoor activates at build/compile or request time
fetches/executes code from a C2 server

entries.name: request
entries.description: Long-running typosquat of `requests`; one of the most-installed accidental imports on PyPI. Various authors have used the name to push installer-stage payloads.

entries.name: python-requests
entries.description: Typosquat of `requests` using the obvious `python-` prefix; multiple malicious uploads under this name historically.

entries.name: twilio-npm
entries.description: Typosquat of twilio that delivered a Discord token stealer on install. Removed within 48h of disclosure.

entries.name: discord.js-user
entries.description: Discord token grabber targeting Discord.js community. Stole user tokens via Electron LocalStorage access and exfiltrated to a Discord webhook controlled by the attacker.

entries.name: actions/checkout@untrusted-ref
entries.description: Pattern weakness: workflow_run / pull_request_target combined with `actions/checkout` using `${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}` checks out attacker-controlled code with secrets in the environment. Documented by GitHub Security Lab as `pwn requests`.

entries.name: fallguys
entries.description: Capitalized on Fall Guys game hype to publish a fake API client that ran a Discord token stealer + clipboard hijacker on install.

entries.name: kinsing-xmrig-image-family
entries.description: Kinsing malware family operated several Docker Hub images targeting exposed Docker daemons (port 2375). Combined XMRig mining, AWS credential theft, and lateral movement into Kubernetes.

entries.name: cupy
entries.description: Homoglyph / case-variant typosquat of the legitimate CuPy CUDA Python package. Exfiltrated environment variables on import; targeted ML developers.

entries.name: mysqlserver
entries.description: Typosquat of the official `mysql` image. Carried an XMRig miner that auto-started on container run. Pulled 1.4M+ times before removal.

entries.name: noice
entries.description: Typosquat of `voice` (and other short-name gems) that exfiltrated environment variables on require. Removed within days of disclosure.

entries.name: python3-dateutil
entries.description: Typosquat of python-dateutil that exfiltrated arbitrary files and SSH keys to an attacker server. Published alongside the jeIlyfish typosquat.

entries.name: jeIlyfish
entries.description: Typosquat of jellyfish using a capital I in place of a lowercase l. Stole SSH keys and env vars; the second-stage payload was retrieved from a remote host on first import.

entries.name: graboid-cryptojacking-worm
entries.description: Unit42 (2019-10-16) documented Graboid, the first-ever cryptojacking WORM spread via Docker Hub images. Malicious images pocosow/centos:7.6.1810 (10,000+ pulls) and gakeaws/nginx:8.9 (6,500+; gakeaws/mysql identical) deploy a Monero (XMRig) miner and self-propagate: a compromised host pulls 4 shell scripts from C2, then randomly picks targets from a list of 2,000+ unsecured Docker daemons and deploys the malicious container remotely.
indicators: spreads via unsecured Docker daemons (exposed API)

entries.name: rest-client
entries.description: Maintainer account compromise. Backdoored versions exfiltrated environment data to a pastebin URL and could eval remote code via a hidden Pastebin fetch on every load. The gem had ~113M downloads at the time.

entries.name: strong_password
entries.description: Maintainer account compromise. v0.0.7 fetched a Pastebin URL and eval'd its content on load, providing a remote-code-execution backdoor for any Rails app that loaded the gem.

entries.name: electron-native-notify
entries.description: First major npm supply-chain attack targeting Electron apps. Compromised package injected into komodo-related wallet builds; exfiltrated wallet seeds to attacker C2.

entries.name: bb-builder
entries.description: Part of the same Komodo Agama wallet supply-chain attack; bb-builder pulled electron-native-notify in as a dependency, indirectly delivering the wallet stealer to downstream wallet builds.

entries.name: bootstrap-sass
entries.description: Maintainer credentials compromised; a backdoored 3.2.0.3 release contained a hidden hook that executed arbitrary code at assets:precompile time, giving CI-time RCE for any Rails build that pulled the gem.

entries.name: colourama
entries.description: British-spelling typosquat of `colorama`; published a clipboard hijacker that replaced cryptocurrency addresses in copied text.

entries.name: flatmap-stream
entries.description: The actual malicious payload injected into event-stream@3.3.6 — a flatmap utility whose minified-and-encrypted release block targeted the Copay bitcoin wallet's electron build, exfiltrating private keys.

entries.name: event-stream
entries.description: Maintainer account compromised; malicious flatmap-stream dependency added to steal cryptocurrency wallets from copay app

entries.name: requests3
entries.description: Originally a legitimate experimental fork, the name is repeatedly registered by attackers between abandoned periods. Treat as suspicious unless installed from the canonical author.

entries.name: eslint-scope
entries.description: Maintainer credentials stolen via reused password; attacker published a backdoored version that exfiltrated npm credentials of any user who installed it. Transitive dependency of eslint-config-airbnb-base, babel-eslint, webpack.

entries.name: xmrig-cryptominer-cluster
entries.description: Aqua Security found 30+ Docker Hub images pulling XMRig cryptominers on `docker run`. Several were typosquats of `xmrig/xmrig`, `docker`, `ubuntu`, etc. Total downloads in the millions before takedowns.

entries.name: ssh-decorator
entries.description: Early PyPI malware: a Python SSH-wrapper decorator that exfiltrated SSH connection credentials (host/user/key/password) to an attacker host. Among the first publicly documented PyPI supply-chain incidents.

entries.name: getcookies
entries.description: Express middleware that added a hidden HTTP-header-activated reverse shell. Reached production via mailparser dependency chain. npm removed the package within 24 hours of report.

entries.name: urllib
entries.description: Typosquat of urllib3 from the SK-CSIRT 2017 wave. PyPI removed the package shortly after disclosure.

entries.name: setup-tools
entries.description: Typosquat of setuptools from the SK-CSIRT 2017 wave. Same payload pattern as ssh-decorator.

entries.name: djago
entries.description: Another Django typosquat from the SK-CSIRT 2017 disclosure. Same payload as diango.

entries.name: diango
entries.description: Typosquat of django from the original SK-CSIRT 10-package PyPI malware discovery. PyPI mirrored package payloads to attacker host at install time.

entries.name: mongose
entries.description: Typosquat of mongoose ORM that ran the same env-exfil postinstall payload as crossenv. Part of the August 2017 hacktask wave.

entries.name: http-proxy.js
entries.description: Typosquat of http-proxy that exfiltrated environment variables on install. Part of the August 2017 hacktask wave (38 packages).

entries.name: d3.js
entries.description: Typosquat of d3 that exfiltrated env via postinstall script. Part of August 2017 hacktask wave; one of the first widely-publicized npm typosquats.

entries.name: crossenv
entries.description: Typosquat of cross-env that ran a postinstall script exfiltrating environment variables to npm.hofmannsven.com. Part of a 38-package typosquat wave by user `hacktask` that triggered the first wave of npm-side anti-typosquat tooling.

entries.name: babelcli
entries.description: Typosquat of babel-cli; part of the August 2017 `hacktask` typosquat wave that also published crossenv, mongose, d3.js, http-proxy.js etc., each carrying an env-variable exfiltration payload.

entries.name: left-pad
entries.description: Maintainer Azer Koculu unpublished left-pad after a dispute with kik over the kik package name. Removal broke thousands of builds including Babel and React. npm subsequently restored the package and changed unpublish policy. This is the foundational npm supply-chain trust event.
